problem_summary: 2017 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy/ICM, "An International Coalition / Population Zero on Mars"). Team 72283 was asked to design a self-sufficient 10,000-person founding colony ("Population Zero") on Mars and evaluate it against three priority factors — income, education, and equality. The four sub-tasks: (1) define quantitative evaluation metrics for the three priorities; (2) choose a real census dataset and design the demographic/occupational composition of Population Zero; (3) build a model for each priority and show their interactions; (4) integrate the three into one global "happiness index" model and stress-test it under additional migration scenarios. The paper closes with a director's-letter policy recommendation.
models_used: Quasi-Markov chain age-cohort population model: 100-state (age 1-100) transition matrix with survival probabilities q_{i,i+1} from US mortality data and a birth row q_{i,1} from US fertility data; one step = one year. Used to test initial age distributions for stability and find equilibrium population (~18,100).
Birth-rate scaling calibration: multiplied empirical US fertility by a tuned parameter of 1.08 to convert population decline into steady growth toward a stable equilibrium.

Dimensional-analysis occupational sizing: chained unit-conversion (2000 kcal/person/day x 10,000 ppl, kcal-per-acre of sweet potatoes, acres-per-farmer = 4.9, Mars 0.6x growth factor) to derive ~2401 farmers, then inflated to 2448 to cover 6-month parental leave.

Ratio/benchmark scaling for every other occupation: health workers = US 10.5% baseline x 2.18 traveler-illness multiplier; teachers from 19:1 student-teacher ratio; police from a fitted civilians-per-officer curve (Reaves data); sanitation from NYC per-capita rate; researchers from Mars Desert Research Station headcount; residual to commerce.

Education-to-GDP regression: linear and quadratic fits of Barro-Lee average-years-of-schooling vs World Bank GDP per capita (R^2 = 0.27 both), with AIC=2710 model selection used to justify keeping BOTH fits as a range.

Income / GDP model: GDP = C + I + G + NX with NX=0 (self-sufficiency) and I~0; tiered government salary structure (tiers A/B/C set at 65% of US private-sector pay) feeding a kcal demand model with willingness-to-pay scaled by salary ratio (2000 + (tier/a)*700).
Gini coefficient via Lorenz curve to measure income equality (computed 0.0336); tax rate derived as government spending / total income (73.6%).

Homophily-driven occupational-segregation Markov simulation: agents tagged by race (5 groups) and job; each year 5% churn; job choice biased by a homophily factor (1.2) and (in the second variant) by salary, run over 50 years.
Dissimilarity index D = (1/2) sum_j |a_j/a - b_j/b| computed pairwise across races to quantify workforce segregation under equal-pay vs stratified-pay regimes.

Integrated happiness index: weighted sum H = w_edu*N_years + w_gdp*GDPproxy + w_ee*(1-gini) + w_se*seproxy, with all proxies normalized to [0,1] (education / 44 from Mincer's model, GDPproxy = CDF of national GDP distribution, seproxy = harmonic mean of (1-D) values).
Empirical weight elicitation: Pew Values Survey responses scored 0-3 and normalized to derive priority weights per income tier, gender, and age subgroup.

Migration stress-test simulations: re-ran the population Markov model under three scenarios (one-time 10k influx, separate trading sister-city, gradual 100-year migration) plus an Earth-evacuation capacity bound from per-capita government spending.
key_techniques: Metrics-first framing: explicitly defined quantitative proxies (GDP, Gini, minimum salary, student-teacher ratio, dissimilarity) for each fuzzy priority BEFORE modeling, directly answering Task 1 and giving every later number a place to land.

Self-consistency cross-validation as the headline credibility move: the income model's GDP ($288.7M) and the independent education-regression GDP ($289.6M) agree to <0.4% — two unrelated routes converging on the same number.
Standardizing heterogeneous metrics onto a common [0,1] scale (CDF transform for GDP, 1-Gini for equality, harmonic mean of 1-D for social equality) so they can be combined in one weighted index.
Deliberate choice of harmonic mean over arithmetic mean for the social-equality proxy, justified by the principle that any single inequality should dominate ('one right doesn't correct a wrong').
Grounding free parameters (priority weights) in real survey data (Pew) rather than inventing them, then sanity-checking the resulting subgroup orderings against intuition.

Explicit tradeoff analysis: forcing Gini to 0 and showing the harmonic-mean social-equality proxy collapses to 0.889 and happiness drops for every subgroup — a clean quantitative argument for permitting small income inequality.
Earth-analog parameter sourcing with stated reasoning (North-Africa/West-Asia farm size as a Mars analog for arid/sparse conditions; traveler illness rate as a disease-risk proxy).
AIC-based model selection, then keeping both candidate fits to present a defensible range rather than overclaiming one fit.
why_it_won: From a judge's seat this paper wins on three fronts. First, completeness with traceability: every one of the four tasks is restated and answered, and every quantity (farmer count, GDP, Gini, education years, leave length, migration capacity) is derived from a cited real data source rather than asserted — for an ICM policy problem, that data-grounding is exactly the discriminator judges reward. Second, the killer validation move: two structurally independent models (kcal-demand income accounting and education-GDP regression) land on $288.7M vs $289.6M GDP. Independent convergence is the single most persuasive credibility signal a modeling paper can show, and judges remember it. Third, genuine model integration plus a real tradeoff finding: most teams bolt three sub-models together; this team fused them into one happiness index and then USED it to discover something non-trivial — that zeroing the Gini actually lowers everyone's happiness because pay equality drives occupational segregation up. That converts a pile of sub-models into a single decision-relevant insight, and it flows straight into a concrete, defensible policy letter (40-60 child/adult split, age-40 cutoff, tiered salaries, 6-month shared leave, 11 years education). The writing carries it: confident, well-organized, with restatement-assumptions-model-validation structure repeated per task.
section_highlights: Per-task structure is the spine: each task opens with "Restatement of the Problem," then "Assumptions and Justifications," then the model, then a "Validation of the Model" subsection — a repeated, judge-friendly rhythm. The one-page Summary Sheet is unusually strong: it walks the reader through all four tasks and front-loads the punchline numbers (Gini=0.0336, GDP/capita ~$28,800 in the top 25%, 25% farmers). Assumptions are numbered and each carries an explicit justification (e.g. Mars 0.6x crop growth, all-American colony because PUMS is US-only). Validation subsections actively defend surprising results (why ~41% farmers and ~38% health workers are not absurd given survival priority). The closing "Policy Recommendation" is written as a formal letter to the Director — correct register for an ICM policy problem — and restates each quantitative finding as an actionable recommendation. Figures (age-cohort trajectories, Lorenz curve, dissimilarity-over-time for equal vs stratified pay, migration population curves) are tied to specific claims rather than decorative.
pitfalls_or_limitations: Weak statistical fit oversold: the education-GDP regression has R^2 = 0.27 (both linear and quadratic), which is poor, yet a single linear projection ($289.6M) is used as the 'independent confirmation' of the income model. The celebrated cross-validation is partly fortunate given that error band.

False precision throughout: numbers like 2401.41 farmers, 886.869 kcal/yr/acre, Gini = 0.0335591, happiness = 0.690827 are reported to absurd significant figures despite resting on rough Earth-analog assumptions.
Many single-source, casual citations (Daily Mail, Wikipedia, a blog 'Farmland LP', CNET) underpin load-bearing parameters (traveler illness 2.18x, kcal per acre, Mars 0.6x growth).
The genetic-variation metric w = 1/prod(p_j) is introduced, called 'crude,' and never actually used in any downstream model — decorative.

Heavy US-data transplant to Mars (US fertility/mortality, US salaries, US Pew survey, all-American population) with little sensitivity analysis on how Martian conditions would shift these; the 1.08 birth multiplier and 1.2 homophily factor are hand-tuned with thin justification.

Some internal inconsistency / sloppiness: farmer count quoted variously as 2401, 2448, and 'about 2400'; the salary vector in the equality sim ([50024.7, 40696.5, 38000]) doesn't match the income section's tier-C value (25,260.3 / 38,862); 2.7 vs 2000 kcal typo in the farmer derivation.
Limited true sensitivity/uncertainty analysis: the model varies salary tiers and tests the Gini=0 case, but does not systematically perturb the many assumed constants to bound the headline outputs.

Happiness-index weights use the SAME four priority categories for every subgroup, so cross-subgroup happiness values differ only modestly (0.68-0.79); the paper concedes this but it limits the integrated model's discriminating power.
reusable_patterns: Define quantitative proxy metrics for every qualitative objective FIRST, then make all later modeling produce or consume those metrics — turns a vague policy prompt into a measurable one.
Independent cross-validation: deliberately derive a key headline quantity by two unrelated methods and report their agreement. This is the highest-leverage credibility technique in the whole paper.

Age-cohort Markov/Leslie-matrix population model: a reusable engine for any demographics problem — survival probabilities on the off-diagonal, fertility on the first row, multiply year-by-year to find equilibrium and test initial distributions.
Dimensional-analysis sizing for resource/workforce questions: chain unit conversions from a fundamental need (kcal/day) to a headcount, documenting each factor and its source.
Benchmark-and-scale for secondary quantities: take a real per-capita or ratio statistic and scale it, applying an explicit, justified multiplier for the novel context (e.g. x2.18 illness risk).
Normalize-then-weight integration: map every sub-model output to [0,1] (CDF transform, 1-x, harmonic mean) and combine in a single weighted index so heterogeneous models become one decision number.
Choose the aggregation operator deliberately (harmonic vs arithmetic mean) and justify it from the problem's value structure.
Source free parameters/weights from real surveys (Pew-style) instead of inventing them, then validate the output ordering against intuition.
Tradeoff/counterfactual experiment: deliberately push one parameter to its extreme (Gini -> 0) and show the integrated objective gets worse — converts a model into a policy argument.
Per-section template (Restate -> Assumptions+Justifications -> Model -> Validation) plus a director's-letter policy close: a clean, repeatable skeleton for ICM policy papers.
Earth-analog selection with explicit reasoning when no native data exists (pick the analog, state why its conditions match).
